Deploy a machine-learning preprocessing pipeline (trained on retrospective publicly available polysomnography datasets) to pre-score respiratory, motor, and arousal segments before human review, with human raters confirming or overriding machine assignments, reducing net annotation time per night by 35–45% but introducing dependency on model generalization to the in-home sensor environment.
levers.review: Controls resource scarcity (staffing and time availability) vs. scientific ambition (richness of the dataset and algorithm sophistication). Weakness: The options fail to consider participant experience—increased sensor burden or delayed feedback may reduce retention in later stays, especially if participants perceive long review delays as lack of interest in their data quality.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Reliability Threshold and Dual-Scoring Intensity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current protocol mandates 100% dual-scoring of all flagged events by two independent raters using AASM criteria, ensuring rigorous adjudication. Systemic: This consumes approximately 20–25% of total scoring FTE and lengthens event adjudication turnaround from 1–2 weeks to 3–4 weeks, reducing the event-labeled dataset available for algorithm training by 30% within the first 12 months and delaying confidence intervals on inter-rater reliability itself until month 8–10. Strategic: Early publication momentum and algorithm iteration speed compete directly with methodological rigor; premature release of publications with weak inter-rater metrics (e.g., <0.70 kappa) jeopardizes phase-two grant credibility, while over-investing in dual-scoring delays proof-of-concept.
options: Maintain dual-scoring for 100% of adjudicated events, accepting a 3–4 week review cycle, with inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa, percentage agreement) published in the first methods paper by month 12, prioritizing methodological transparency over early quantitative output.

Implement a risk-stratified dual-scoring strategy: tier-1 clear-consensus events (confidence ≥0.90 on technician flagging) receive single-rater review with 10% spot-check by second rater; tier-2 ambiguous events (confidence 0.60–0.90) receive full dual-scoring; tier-3 borderline non-events receive provisional single-rater assessment, reducing overall dual-scoring volume by 40% and accelerating algorithm training cohort assembly to month 6–7.

Adopt a sequential-review model in which a primary rater scores all flagged events and a second rater independently scores only disagreement cases or a randomly sampled 20% validation subset, reducing dual-scoring FTE by 60% and cutting adjudication turnaround to 10–14 days, with inter-rater reliability quantified through the smaller validation subset and acknowledged as a conservative estimate.
levers.review: Controls methodological rigor (confidence in event adjudication) vs. operational velocity (time to actionable algorithm dataset and publication readiness). Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of inter-rater disagreement patterns on algorithm development—if disagreements cluster around specific event morphologies or sensor modalities, a thin validation subset may miss systematic bias that undermines algorithm generalization.

levers.name: Participant Retention Incentive and Extended-Stay Encouragement Structure
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current per-night compensation is €80 plus meals (approximately €560 per week for a typical 7-night stay), with zero-event stays eligible for 2-week extensions at the same rate. Systemic: Retention for zero-event participants is estimated at 30–40% for standard stays and 50–60% for extension weeks; raising incentive to €100/night increases extension participation to 75–85%, requiring an additional €50K–70K annually but yielding 8–12 additional participant-weeks and capturing 3–5 extra events, a net gain of 15–20% event yield over 12 months. Strategic: The unit must choose between flat-rate equity (all participants compensated equally) and outcome-based incentivization (rewarding longer participation or event-rich stays), balancing budget efficiency against participant fairness and potential recruitment reputation effects if selective incentives become known.
options: Maintain flat €80/night compensation for all participant nights with explicit communication that extended stays are offered solely to maximize individual participant data quality and scientific yield, not as preferential reward, positioning retention as scientific necessity rather than financial incentive.

Introduce a tiered incentive structure: €80/night for standard stays, €100/night for participants who consent to extension weeks (signaling research value), and a €500 completion bonus for any participant providing ≥5 captured-and-adjudicated events, targeting approximately 40–50% of the cohort and adding €8K–12K to annual participant-compensation budget.

Develop a participant-choice menu in which admissions staff present three options during intake: standard stay at €80/night with no commitment to extension, committed-research option at €95/night with a pre-agreed willingness to extend if zero events emerge, or intensive-capture option at €110/night reserved for participants who self-report ≥4 episodes per month and accept protocol escalations (e.g., additional PSG nights), allowing self-selection into economic models matching risk tolerance.
levers.review: Controls budget predictability (flat vs. variable compensation) vs. data-yield optimization (event capture rates and extended-stay participation). Weakness: The options fail to consider ethical concerns about financial coercion—higher incentives for longer stays or more intensive protocols may inadvertently recruit financially vulnerable participants willing to accept greater risk, raising informed consent and equity concerns.

levers.name: External Data-Sharing Governance and Academic-Industry Collaboration Scope
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current policy restricts public sharing to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements, with video withheld entirely, and pharma partnerships confined to a 30-day pre-publication comment window with no data access. Systemic: This limits external tool development and accelerates academic adoption but forgoes approximately €200K–400K in potential annual pharma partnership revenue (if a company pays for a 2-year data license and collaborative algorithm development), reducing year-two budget flexibility and external validation opportunities. Strategic: The unit must balance scientific autonomy and publication primacy against financial sustainability and faster algorithm validation; opening data to external validators accelerates confidence in findings but risks loss of first-mover advantage and potential misuse of controlled datasets in commercial diagnostic products.
options: Maintain current restrictive policy: deposit de-identified physiological time series and annotated event metadata in a public repository under CC-BY-NC license (non-commercial), reserve video and granular sensor metadata for approved academic collaborators under formal data agreements, and decline pharma partnerships, ensuring full publication control and long-term scientific primacy.

Establish a tiered-access framework: Tier 1 (public repository) releases anonymized polysomnography montages and event timelines; Tier 2 (academic partnership) offers fuller sensor streams and inter-rater reliability data under collaborative publication agreements; Tier 3 (industry partnership) provides pharma with access to the full de-identified dataset, annotations, and algorithm development environment under a company-funded partnership model with a 6-month publication embargo and a clause reserving academic publication rights, generating €150K–250K in year-two revenue.

Invite a pharma strategic partner from protocol inception, offering early-stage data and algorithm prototypes in exchange for joint governance of the repository and shared intellectual property on event-triage tools (50% patent ownership by university, 50% by pharma), with a licensing-revenue share (20% to university) if the tool is commercialized; this secures €500K–800K in direct partnership funding but subordinates publication timeline to partner approval and risks academic independence.
levers.review: Controls intellectual property and publication autonomy (academic independence) vs. financial runway and external validation speed (partnership funding and broader testing). Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of data leakage or misuse by external validators—even under strict agreements, sharing physiological and event-annotation data with industry entities creates ongoing surveillance and liability exposure if a partner breaches confidentiality or uses data in ways not explicitly anticipated in the original consent.

levers.name: Algorithm Deployment Maturity and Translational-Medicine Pathway Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current protocol specifies a semi-automated event-triage tool benchmarked against dual human scoring, with no claim of autonomous diagnosis and explicit framing as a research aid, not a clinical tool. Systemic: Restricting deployment to research use (within the unit and academic collaborators) caps user base at <100 institutions within 5 years and requires <€50K in regulatory and support infrastructure; authorizing limited clinical deployment (e.g., offering the algorithm as a stand-alone annotation tool to sleep labs under a 'research use only' exemption letter) expands addressable market to >500 institutions and generates €5K–10K annual SaaS subscription revenue per lab, totaling €150K–300K annually by year 3, but introduces liability exposure and ongoing compliance obligations.
options: Keep the algorithm as a research methodology tool integrated into published methods papers, release code and weights under an open-source academic license (GPL or CC-BY-NC), and explicitly forbid commercial deployment or claims of clinical utility, maximizing reproducibility and academic impact while maintaining zero regulatory burden.

Pursue a low-friction clinical-research-tool pathway: package the algorithm as a standalone annotation support system for sleep labs, file a 510(k) premarket notification or equivalent EU In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation notice with regulatory bodies positioning it as a 'clinical decision support aid'—not autonomous diagnosis—requiring a €30K–60K regulatory effort and annual post-market surveillance, enabling licensed deployment to 50–100 sleep labs within 3 years under a subscription model generating €100K–150K annual recurring revenue.

Establish a independent spin-off company (university-owned minority stake, 30–40%) to commercialize the algorithm as a diagnostic-support platform with full regulatory clearance (IVDR CE marking or FDA 510(k)), scaling to 200+ institutions over 5 years and generating €500K–1M annual revenue by year 5; this requires €300K–500K upfront investment for regulatory work, business operations, and marketing, but detaches the parent research unit from liability and accelerates external validation through paying customers.
levers.review: Controls regulatory and liability exposure (research-only vs. clinical deployment) vs. financial sustainability and external impact (market reach and revenue generation). Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory and reputational risk of misclassification—if the algorithm is promoted as a 'decision support aid' but used by sleep labs as a primary diagnostic tool without adequate human oversight, adverse outcomes may trigger regulatory action and litigation, regardless of intended use language, exposing both the university and any partner company.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts that are specific to this project's domain (e.g., "[Domain]-[Decision Type] Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions

Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
